title: Residential Duct Install

components.name: Flex duct run — 12 feet
components.violation: Flex duct exceeds 5-foot maximum run length per SMACNA standards. Excessive flex creates turbulence and pressure drop.

components.name: Return air — no filter rack
components.violation: Return air grille has no filter rack. All return air must be filtered before entering the air handler per mechanical code.

components.name: Duct supported by wire
components.violation: Ductwork must be supported by trapeze hangers or straps, not wire. Wire hangers can cut into insulation and don't provide adequate support.

explanation: This residential duct system has 3 code violations that would fail inspection. Proper duct support, filtration, and flex duct length limits are fundamental requirements.

title: Rooftop Unit Installation

components.name: No vibration isolation pads
components.violation: Rooftop units must have vibration isolation to prevent noise and vibration transmission to the building structure. Required by ASHRAE and most building codes.

components.name: Gas line without drip leg
components.violation: Gas piping to equipment must have a sediment trap (drip leg) to catch moisture and debris before it enters the gas valve. Prevents valve damage and unsafe operation.

components.name: Return air opening without bird screen
components.violation: Outdoor air intakes and return air openings must have corrosion-resistant screens (min 1/4" mesh) to prevent entry of birds, rodents, and large debris.

explanation: Rooftop installations have unique requirements for weather protection, vibration, and gas safety that differ from indoor equipment.

title: Residential Furnace Setup

components.name: PVC vent pipe with proper slope
No combustion air intake
components.violation: Gas furnaces in confined spaces require combustion air openings — one high and one low — to ensure adequate air for safe combustion. Without it, the furnace can produce CO.

components.name: Flue pipe with downward slope
components.violation: Single-wall vent connectors must slope upward at least 1/4" per foot toward the chimney or vent terminal. Downward slope traps condensate and prevents proper draft.

explanation: Furnace installations require proper venting, combustion air, and gas safety. These are life-safety issues that directly affect carbon monoxide risk.

title: Commercial AHU Ductwork

components.name: Fire damper at rated wall penetration
Duct through fire wall — no fire damper
components.violation: All duct penetrations through fire-rated walls and floors must have fire dampers rated to match the wall rating. This is a life-safety code requirement.

components.name: Duct insulation with exposed fiberglass
components.violation: Duct insulation in occupied plenums must have a proper jacket/facing. Exposed fiberglass releases fibers into the airstream causing IAQ issues.

components.name: Branch duct aspect ratio 6:1
components.violation: Rectangular duct aspect ratio should not exceed 4:1 for efficiency. Higher ratios increase friction loss and are difficult to seal properly.

explanation: Commercial ductwork has stricter fire safety, IAQ, and efficiency standards than residential. Fire dampers are one of the most commonly missed items in inspections.

title: Mini-Split Installation

components.name: Refrigerant lines without insulation
components.violation: Suction line (large line) must be insulated to prevent condensation and maintain efficiency. Uninsulated lines lose capacity and drip moisture causing water damage.

components.name: Outdoor unit on pad with clearance
Line set routed through wall with sleeve
Condensate line draining uphill
components.violation: Condensate must drain by gravity (1/4" per foot minimum slope) or use a condensate pump. Uphill drainage will cause water backup and overflow from the indoor unit.

explanation: Mini-split installations are increasingly common. The two most frequent issues are uninsulated refrigerant lines and improper condensate drainage.

title: VAV Box Installation

components.name: No airflow sensor ring
components.violation: VAV boxes require an airflow measuring station (sensor ring or averaging pitot tube) to measure actual airflow for proper modulation. Without it, the controller cannot maintain setpoint.

components.name: Reheat coil piped without isolation valves
components.violation: Hot water reheat coils must have isolation valves for maintenance and balancing. Without them, the entire zone piping loop must be drained for any coil work.

explanation: VAV systems are the backbone of commercial HVAC. Proper instrumentation and piping accessories are critical for energy efficiency and maintainability.

title: Bathroom Exhaust Fan

components.name: Exhaust duct terminated in attic
components.violation: Exhaust ducts must terminate outdoors, never in an attic, crawl space, or soffit. Moisture buildup in attics causes mold, wood rot, and structural damage.

components.name: 4" flex duct run — 25 feet with 3 bends
components.violation: Long flex duct runs with multiple bends severely reduce airflow. Each 90° bend in 4" flex adds ~15 equivalent feet of duct. This run has excessive pressure drop.

explanation: Bathroom exhaust is critical for moisture control. The two most common failures are terminating in the attic and excessive duct runs that kill airflow.

title: Chiller Plant Piping

components.name: Chilled water pipe — no insulation vapor barrier
components.violation: Chilled water pipes MUST have vapor barrier on insulation to prevent condensation. Without it, moisture penetrates insulation, causes mold, and reduces R-value to near zero.

components.name: No dielectric union at steel-to-copper transition
components.violation: Dissimilar metals (steel to copper) require dielectric unions to prevent galvanic corrosion. Without them, the pipe joint will corrode and fail within 2-5 years.

components.name: Flow meter on chilled water return

explanation: Chiller plant piping requires careful attention to insulation vapor barriers and dissimilar metal protection. These failures are expensive to fix after installation.

title: Thermostat Wiring Job

components.name: Y wire (cool) — Blue
components.violation: Y (cooling) wire should be Yellow, not Blue. Blue is reserved for C (common) wire. Incorrect color coding causes confusion during service calls and violates industry standard.

components.name: Thermostat wire running parallel to 120V Romex
components.violation: Low-voltage thermostat wire must maintain separation from line-voltage wiring. Running parallel causes electromagnetic interference that can damage the thermostat or cause erratic operation.

components.name: Wire nuts joining thermostat wires
components.violation: Thermostat wire connections should use proper terminal blocks or crimp connectors, not wire nuts. Wire nuts on 18 AWG thermostat wire often lose connection over time due to vibration.

explanation: Thermostat wiring crosses HVAC and electrical trades. Color coding, separation from high-voltage, and proper connections are critical for reliable operation.

title: Kitchen Hood Exhaust

components.name: Type I hood over cooking equipment
Exhaust duct — PVC material
components.violation: Kitchen exhaust ducts must be steel (min 16 gauge) or stainless steel. PVC will melt from grease-laden exhaust heat and is a severe fire hazard.

components.name: No makeup air unit
components.violation: Kitchen exhaust systems require makeup air to replace exhausted air. Without it, the kitchen goes negative pressure causing back-drafting of gas appliances (CO hazard) and door operation issues.

components.name: Fan on roof with hinged curb

explanation: Commercial kitchen ventilation is one of the most heavily regulated HVAC systems due to fire and CO risks. Material selection and makeup air are non-negotiable.

title: Window AC Unit Setup

components.name: Unit sized for room square footage
Mounted level (front-to-back)
components.violation: Window AC units must tilt slightly outward (about 1/4" to 1/2") to allow condensate to drain outside. A perfectly level unit will pool water inside and potentially leak into the room.

components.name: Unit plugged into extension cord
components.violation: Window AC units must be plugged directly into a wall outlet, never an extension cord. Extension cords cause voltage drop, overheating, and are a fire hazard with high-amperage equipment.

explanation: Even simple window units have safety and performance requirements. Proper tilt for drainage and direct electrical connection are commonly overlooked.

title: Clean Room HVAC

components.name: Standard fiberglass filters only
components.violation: Clean rooms require HEPA (99.97% @ 0.3 micron) or ULPA filtration. Standard fiberglass filters are pre-filters only and cannot achieve the particle counts required for any clean room classification.

components.name: Ductwork with internal liner
components.violation: Clean room ductwork must NOT have internal fibrous liner. Fibers shed into the airstream and contaminate the clean space. Use smooth galvanized or stainless steel duct.

explanation: Clean room HVAC is a specialty requiring understanding of particle filtration, pressurization, and material selection. Journeyman-level knowledge.

title: Union Hall TAB Training: Air Balance

components.name: Using calibrated manometer for static pressure
Single-point reading at duct center
components.violation: SMACNA TAB procedures require a pitot tube traverse (multiple readings across the duct cross-section) to determine average velocity. A single center-point reading overestimates airflow by 10-25% because velocity is highest at the center of the duct.

components.name: Damper left unlocked after final balance
components.violation: All balancing dampers must be locked in their final position after TAB is complete. Unlocked dampers are easily bumped or moved by other trades, invalidating the entire balance.
components.codeReference: SMACNA TAB Procedural Guide / AABC Standards

components.name: Balancing hood used for diffuser CFM measurement
No balancing report form being used
components.violation: All TAB work must be documented on proper report forms recording design CFM, measured CFM, and final adjusted CFM for every outlet. Without documentation, the work cannot be verified or submitted to the engineer for approval.

explanation: Testing, Adjusting, and Balancing (TAB) is a core SMACNA apprenticeship skill. Proper traverse readings, damper locking, and documentation are the three pillars of professional TAB work taught at every union hall.

title: Union Hall Safety: Sheet Metal Shop Hazards

components.name: Safety glasses worn at all times
Handling raw sheet metal without cut-resistant gloves
components.violation: Raw sheet metal edges are razor-sharp and cause severe lacerations. OSHA and SMACNA safety standards require cut-resistant gloves (ANSI A4 or higher) when handling unfinished sheet metal. This is the #1 injury in sheet metal shops.

components.name: Hearing protection worn near brake press
Plasma cutter used without welding screen
components.violation: Plasma cutting produces intense UV/IR radiation and bright arc flash that causes eye damage (arc eye) and skin burns. A welding screen or curtain must be deployed around the cutting area to protect the operator and nearby workers.

components.name: Pittsburgh seam machine guard bypassed
components.violation: Pittsburgh lock seam machines have pinch points that can amputate fingers. Machine guards are required by OSHA and must never be bypassed or removed. SMACNA apprenticeship training specifically emphasizes: never defeat a machine guard.

components.name: Steel-toed boots worn on shop floor

explanation: Sheet metal fabrication shops are high-hazard environments. SMACNA union apprenticeship programs dedicate significant training to shop safety because hand lacerations, eye injuries, and machine entanglement are the most common incidents. Safety is the first lesson at every union hall.

title: Flex Duct Installation at Wall Penetration

components.name: Flex duct run measuring 8 feet total length
components.violation: Flexible duct is limited to a maximum of 5 feet in length per NFPA 90A. An 8-foot run exceeds this limit, increases pressure drop, and reduces airflow performance significantly.

components.name: Flex duct penetrating a fire-rated wall assembly
components.violation: Flexible duct is not permitted to penetrate fire-rated wall or floor assemblies. Only rigid metal duct with proper fire dampers may pass through rated barriers. Flex duct will burn through and compromise the fire barrier.

components.name: Insulated flex duct with vapor barrier intact
Proper clamp connections at both ends
Return air grille with filter rack

explanation: NFPA 90A strictly limits flex duct to 5-foot maximum lengths and prohibits penetration through fire-rated assemblies. These are UCSB spec requirements that protect building compartmentalization during a fire.

title: Plenum Duct Insulation

components.name: Supply duct sealed with mastic at joints
Duct insulation in plenum space with flame spread rating of 30
components.violation: Duct insulation installed in a plenum space must have a flame spread index of 25 or less and a smoke developed index of 50 or less per NFPA 90A. A rating of 30 exceeds the maximum and creates a fire hazard in the plenum.

components.name: Access panels provided at fire damper locations
Insulation properly secured with weld pins and speed clips
Plenum-rated cable ties used for sensor wiring

explanation: Plenum spaces have strict material requirements because they serve as air pathways. NFPA 90A requires insulation with a flame spread index no greater than 25. Materials that exceed this limit can rapidly spread fire through the building via the plenum.

title: Kitchen Exhaust Fire Protection

components.name: Type I commercial kitchen exhaust hood
Grease duct constructed of 16-gauge welded steel
Fire barrier wrap on grease duct only 1 inch thick
components.violation: Fire barrier wrap protecting kitchen exhaust grease ducts must be a minimum of 1.5 inches thick per ASTM E2336 / UL 2221 fire-resistance testing. A 1-inch wrap does not provide the required fire endurance rating to prevent fire spread from a grease duct fire.

components.name: Exhaust fan on roof with proper curb
Cleanout access doors at every direction change
Fire suppression system nozzles in hood

explanation: Kitchen exhaust grease ducts are a significant fire hazard. UCSB specs require fire barrier wrap to meet ASTM E2336 testing at a minimum 1.5-inch thickness. Inadequate wrap thickness will not contain a grease fire within the duct, allowing it to spread to the building structure.

title: UCSB Residential Unit Diffuser Installation

components.name: Supply diffuser SA12X8-375 in living room ceiling
EA6X6-100 exhaust grille serving full bathroom (100 CFM required)
components.violation: The EA6X6 grille is rated for only 100 CFM, which matches the requirement. However, the 6x6 grille is at its absolute maximum capacity with zero margin. Per the UCSB diffuser schedule, the EA6X6-100 operates at 0.15" WG pressure drop at 100 CFM — any duct restriction will cause underperformance.

components.name: EA6X6-100 exhaust grille serving kitchen (270 CFM range hood required)
components.violation: The EA6X6 is rated for only 100 CFM maximum, but the kitchen range hood requires 270 CFM exhaust per the UCSB plans. This grille is massively undersized — it should be at minimum an EA10X10-400 or dedicated range hood exhaust connection.

components.name: Return air grille RA22X22-1520 in hallway ceiling
Diffuser pressure drop measured at 0.12" WG
Trickle vent providing 45 CFM fresh air

explanation: This residential unit has an undersized kitchen exhaust grille. The EA6X6 at 100 CFM cannot serve a 270 CFM range hood — the velocity through the grille would be nearly 3x the design limit, creating extreme noise and back-pressure that would render the range hood ineffective. Kitchen exhaust must be sized for the specific appliance CFM requirement.

title: UCSB Mechanical Room Ventilation

components.name: 14"x44" EA duct for mechanical room exhaust
38"x18" OA duct for outside air intake
Exhaust louver with 2.0 sq ft free area serving 3,000 CFM
components.violation: Per the UCSB louver schedule, 3,000 CFM requires a minimum of 6 sq ft free area. A 2.0 sq ft louver at 3,000 CFM creates 1,500 FPM face velocity — three times the 500 FPM maximum. This causes rain entrainment, excessive noise, and the exhaust fan cannot overcome the louver resistance.

components.name: C6x8.2 structural channel supports for duct hangers
Generator room exhaust outlet 8 feet above grade
components.violation: Generator room exhaust must terminate a minimum of 10 feet above grade per the UCSB mechanical plans. At 8 feet, exhaust fumes containing carbon monoxide and diesel particulates are too close to pedestrian level and can be drawn into adjacent building openings.

components.name: Generator room with 72 sq ft free area opening
Generator exhaust outlet 12 feet from property line

explanation: This mechanical room installation has two violations: the exhaust louver is severely undersized (2 sq ft vs. required 6 sq ft for 3,000 CFM), and the generator exhaust terminates only 8 feet above grade instead of the required 10 feet minimum. Both violations affect life safety — inadequate louver sizing prevents proper ventilation, and low generator exhaust creates toxic fume exposure risks.

title: UCSB Student Housing Duct Sizing

components.name: Main supply trunk 32"x32" SA duct serving corridor
Branch SA duct 12"x8" to unit delivering 375 CFM
EA duct 8"x8" serving shared bathroom at 150 CFM
components.violation: The EA8X8 grille is rated for 200 CFM per the schedule, but a shared bathroom requires 150 CFM. While the grille capacity is sufficient, the 8x8 duct size is acceptable. The actual violation is using this for a shared bathroom — shared bathrooms serving 3+ students need 150 CFM, and the duct/grille must be verified for this flow. However, the grille connection at the duct is actually correct here.

components.name: Return air duct 10"x10" serving 1,520 CFM common area
components.violation: A 10"x10" duct has only 100 sq inches of cross section. At 1,520 CFM, duct velocity would be approximately 2,190 FPM — far exceeding the 1,200 FPM maximum for occupied-space return ducts. The plans call for a 22"x22" RA duct (RA22X22-1520) with 484 sq inches providing approximately 450 FPM velocity.

components.name: Kitchen makeup air shaft providing 300 CFM
Transfer air grille between bedroom and hallway at 150 CFM
EA16X16-1150 exhaust grille serving laundry room at 1,150 CFM
Exhaust riser 6"x6" serving 1,900 CFM from lower floors
components.violation: A 6"x6" duct has only 36 sq inches. At 1,900 CFM, velocity would be approximately 7,600 FPM — catastrophically high. The plans show EA60X16-1900 for this application, requiring a 60"x16" duct (960 sq inches) to maintain acceptable velocity around 285 FPM.

explanation: This student housing duct installation has two critical sizing errors: a return air duct undersized by a factor of nearly 5x (10x10 vs. 22x22), and an exhaust riser undersized by a factor of over 25x (6x6 vs. 60x16). Both would create extreme noise, air starvation, and fan failure. Duct sizing must always match the plans — cross-referencing duct dimensions with CFM requirements catches these errors before installation.
